title: stop building Agents and start building something that lasts.

eval.summary: Jake Van Clief argues that AI agents are overbuilt and that the best 'agent' is a markdown file plus scripts routed through that file, organized via folder hierarchies. He cites Anthropic's Claude Code as validation — noting Anthropic doesn't build a visual agent platform, instead shipping a markdown-driven CLI. The core thesis: stop chasing agent frameworks and start building durable, file-system-grounded workflows. He claims 8 years Marine Corps cryptography/intelligence background and consulting work with KPMG. The content is short, credential-heavy, and light on implementation detail. The practical takeaway is essentially: CLAUDE.md + scripts + folder structure = sufficient agent architecture. This is directionally consistent with how Alan OS is already built (CLAUDE.md as working memory, FastAPI scripts, n8n workflows, no LangChain). There is no new tool, repo, or technique introduced — just a framing argument. No token efficiency data, no API surface, no integration path. The video validates existing Veritas architectural choices but adds zero new capability.
eval.fit_rationale: Validates the existing Alan OS architecture (CLAUDE.md + scripts + FastAPI) but introduces no new tool, technique, or integration path.

stack_evaluation.replaces_or_complements: nothing — validates existing stack choices, adds no new capability
stack_evaluation.reasoning: Fails P3 outright — no measurable token efficiency improvement, no eval rubric, no quantitative claim. Fails P7 implicitly — the 'technique' (CLAUDE.md + scripts) is already the canonical Alan OS pattern, so overlap is ~100% with no extension value. The video is a credential-heavy framing argument, not an implementable artifact.
